paragraphs.text: VERY early in the sixteenth century there was published in
a book of romance called "Las Sergas de Esplandian." In this book
, where a great abundance of gold and precious stone is found." As far as can be known it was in this book that the name "California" was first coined. And from that hour the quest of the
began—the goal of deep-sea wanderers and soldiers of fortune, conquistadores, proselytizers and the dreamers of dreams.

was an island lasted long after her golden shores of glory had been seen and to some extent explored. Legend also peopled it with a race of Amazons who wore bracelets and other ornaments of gold. It was pictured as
, which, of course, it was and is, but the discovery of gold remained for the Americans who did not come in the footsteps of the Spaniards until more than three hundred years had passed.

Very few of the California Indians occupied a plane of civilization higher than that of beasts when the white men first found them. Some, it is true, were a little more intelligent than others. For instance, the "
," who lived in the vicinity of
, built rude shelters for themselves in the shape of huts.
had some skill in the making of implements and in some instances really fashioned a sort of pretty jewelry from shells and the bones and claws of animals. Occasionally
were found who fished and navigated in a small way with rude canoes that they had somehow learned to construct. Like all Indians, the world over, they used bows and arrows, and the
men of some of the tribes
were very skil[l]ful archers in war and in the hunt. Perhaps the best fighting men among them were found in the

was not such as to fascinate an artist in quest of types of beauty. Men and women alike were usually below the average height of human beings. They were fat and ungainly, with abnormal abdomens but thin shrunken legs, the result, no doubt, of an almost nameless diet. They ate anything they could lay their hands on, including bugs, lizards, grub worms, grasshoppers, carrion and raw fish. It made no difference to them in what state of decay these things were found, they ate them. They had straight coarse black hair, low foreheads, small eyes and wide flat noses on wide flat faces.

They had no names for themselves, no traditions and no religion. They were lazy and indolent to a degree and made no attempt whatever to till the soil. In their dealings with the white men they were much given to petty thievery and treachery. On occasion they committed murder. The lives they led subjected them to many diseases. Such a thing as a marriage relation appears to have been almost wholly unknown among them and there was no such thing as morals.
were the intellectual superiors of those who dwelt on the
Concerning them a pathetic tale is told which belongs to later years yet which furnishes a vivid picture of the manner in which they must have existed. It is the story of "

differing in language and in customs from the Indians of the mainland. They lived as in a world by themselves and were seldom, if ever, visited by their kinsmen from across
for the reason that the Indians of California were not a sea-faring people. The most they ever did in the way of seamanship was to venture short distances from shore on rafts constructed of tules.
There came a day when it was decided to transport the
in order that they might be under the more constant care of
, who desired, of course, to civilize and christianize them. So,
was gathered together and put on board.
, which in the excitement she had forgotten. As she did not return in a reasonable length of time, and a great storm having come up,
sailed without her, doubtless with the intention of returning later.
never returned, nor any other ship for eighteen years. At the end of those years—a generation—
saw a strange sight. Awaiting them at the water's edge was
that resembled nothing so much as a huge human bird. It was
the forgotten woman of San Nicolas
clad in a robe of feathers which she had woven from the wings and backs of wild birds and sea-fowl.
who lived so long alone on
make the experience of Robinson Crusoe seem easily plausible.
and one of these appears to have been the only companion

had. She had made a hut of whalebones, covered it with brush and had built a brush fence around it to shelter her little home from the winds of the sea. She had a plentiful supply of food from abalone and other fish. She was a skil[l]ful weaver and had made many baskets from grass fiber. Her method of killing seal was to hunt them at night, stealing up to them and killing them with stones. Her fish-lines were made from flesh of seals and her hooks from abalone shells. She had become very skil[l]ful in catching birds.
managed to capture her, but once captured she became very friendly and as playful as a child.
was found to have built a screen to shield the eyes of a young otter from the sun, thus proving her gentleness of heart.
she was much terrified at the sight of
and other things connected with the ways of white men which she had never seen before or of which she had never heard.
brought Indians from far and near in an effort to understand the woman's speech, but it was all in vain. No one could grasp the meaning of a word she spoke.
Very kind were all the people of
the lost woman of San Nicolas
, but in six weeks she sickened and died.
, who had accomplished her capture and in whose household she had been so tenderly cared for, presented
with all her household implements, her baskets and grass bottles and her birdskin dresses. In turn
